policy_statement: Streamline the Valle del Piave medium network to reduce congestion and encourage renewable generation.
mechanism_description: Streamlining the electricity distribution network reduces congestion and helps enable more renewable electricity production to be delivered through the grid.
primary_causal_pathway: Grid infrastructure upgrade → ↑ renewable integration / ↓ congestion & curtailment → ↓ fossil generation → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: The electricity distribution network is upgraded/streamlined (e.g., reconductoring, voltage optimization, new feeders/substations, automation) to reduce congestion and increase hosting capacity for renewables.  
(2) Mediating process: Reduced network constraints lower renewable curtailment and speed/enable interconnection, improving dispatchability and delivering more low-carbon kWh through the grid.  
(3) Outcome: Greater renewable penetration displaces marginal fossil generation, reducing CO₂ (and other) emissions from electricity supply.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is enabling additional renewable electricity delivery that displaces fossil-fired generation, thereby reducing GHG emissions from the power system.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Grid upgrades → more renewable electricity delivered → less fossil generation → lower GHG emissions.  
2) Why Mitigation over alternatives: While upgrades can improve reliability (adaptation co-benefit) and reduce losses (resource efficiency co-benefit), the described mechanism’s main climate impact is decarbonizing electricity by enabling renewable integration.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a “grid upgrade” were mainly for storm-hardening or outage reduction, Adaptation could dominate; if primarily for loss reduction without affecting generation mix, Resource Efficiency could dominate. Those are not the stated mechanism here.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances of this mechanism: Any policy framed as network upgrades to reduce congestion and enable renewable delivery shares the same primary mitigation pathway via fossil displacement.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment is deferred to policy-level evidence: details like whether the main effect is curtailment reduction, interconnection acceleration, or loss reduction require policy-specific evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential ambiguity when “network upgrade” is motivated chiefly by resilience/hardening or by technical loss reduction; classification here assumes the dominant intent/effect is renewable integration and resulting grid decarbonization, per the mechanism description.
key_indicators: "streamlining of the ... medium network"; "reduce congestion"; "encourage production from renewable sources"
co_benefits: Improved grid reliability/quality of supply; reduced losses and operating costs; potential local air-quality improvements from reduced fossil generation
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a planning/strategy measure if the underlying action is purely organizational (e.g., operational reconfiguration) rather than capital works, but the wording aligns most with grid infrastructure upgrade.
